speaker: interviewer
text: 1996. We are on tape number 2. The interview is with Alfred Traum. Tell me about your bicycle experience.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I, I mentioned that I really enjoyed riding bicycles. And I had to rent them. Now, the bikes-- the rental place was inside a park. And by this time, parks were already out of bounds to Jews. And-- but I had been going there for a long time, so I used to go through there. On one occasion, I thought I had lost my money, and I got pretty upset. I was looking all over. I thought I'd lost it on the floor. And an old fellow came over, sort of a grandpa type. Seemed to be a nice man. And he asked me what the problem was. And I told him I thought I'd lost my money. And he started putting his arm around me, started giving me a lecture on, little boy, Hitler wouldn't be happy if he saw me crying. He was giving me this real Nazi propaganda speech. And it so happened I found the money caught in the seam of my pocket. And so as I felt that, I brightened up. And he probably thought it was due to his talk. But the reason I bring it up is because people tend to think that these fanatic Nazis were all a rowdy rabble. They were these nice old guys, too. And so I went and got my bicycle. And I came home. And my dad was-- [INAUDIBLE] [CRYING] He was waiting at the window. [CRYING] And I guess you can tell I have very strong feelings [CRYING] towards my father. [CRYING] I'm OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was waiting at the window for you to get home.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I'd ride up and down, no hands, and all that kind of thing and, and take it back, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were about eight years old, nine years old at this time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you go to this school? How many years?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this particular school, I continued until the day, I-- the day before I left for, for England. Now, there's one thing other about life in, in Vienna for me and others like me that I haven't mentioned is that because of the, the pressures of Nazism on one side, you had a, a cohesion of Jewishness balancing on the other side. And so Zionist youth groups suddenly became swelled with more interest and more people. I was too young for it, but my sister used to go. And so I used to hang around them. I sang in a choir. And then, then there was a choir formed by a Jewish organization that-- sort of to counterbalance the Vienna Boys' Choir. And I remember having auditions. And I, I had a pretty, a good voice and the real child soprano type. And so I joined that choir. And they were going to outfit us with all these little sailor suits. And I think that must have happened after I left. But we did give some concerts, and they did make some records. But all this took place in the inner city, which was quite a long way from me. And I used to have to travel by trams to get there and get home in the evening-- usually late in the evening. And the strange thing is that you had this kind of viciousness on one side because you were Jewish. On the other hand, as far as kids going anywhere and traveling freely around town, that was perfectly safe. The type of problems that face us today didn't exist then.

speaker: interviewer
text: Interesting. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In your childhood, you had a lot of friends who were not Jewish. Did any of those friends at any point turn on you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Nobody turned on me. The only, the only thing I can say is that I could register that they felt uncomfortable in my presence. They felt uncomfortable in not coming to my defense when things were said. And I think they avoided me.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Are there any other experiences about your childhood or about anti-Semitism in Vienna that you want to share?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think some of the things became apparent to me only later years. For example, when one of our neighbors, who used to come in to listen to these speeches-- and he was enlisted into some organization. And he had a gas mask. They used to have these round canisters. And apparently, all the people there were issued gas masks but not, not us, not the Jews. When I came to England, one of the first things I got was a gas mask.

speaker: interviewer
text: Interesting. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So then what happened? How did you get connected with the transport?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe it was through a business associate of my father's, and they went to England. And their name was Stary. And they made some contacts. And somehow, the people that took my sister and I in were approached. And they said that they would be willing to take two children as refugees. And then since we had sponsors, they attached us to this Kindertransport. And arrangements were made, and I have, I had a passport and a, a visa from England to be able to enter the country. And so in our case, we already knew where we were going. Most of the kids didn't know-- they-- on the transport. They came there to some kind of a, a center-- adoption center. Then they would-- many of them were billeted out to various hostels and-- but in, in our particular case, we had a home to go to.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your sister with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you find out you were leaving?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we knew [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] some time before-- a few months before-- that some negotiations were going on. Letters were being passed forward. I had to go and have a pictures taken for a passport. And I remember, with my mother, lining up a long time, waiting for permits and waiting for visas and all these things. So these things took quite a long time. So leading up to the actual day of departure must have been several months.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did friends go with you from school? Were they on the same transport?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No friends. The only thing is the, the professor who taught my class-- his son, who was only five years old, was on this transport. So I remember at the station he asked me if I would keep an eye on him. And he sat with me until we arrived to England. I don't know whatever happened to him after that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember leaving?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, clearly. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My-- I think I mentioned my father was a amateur photographer. So we went downstairs in the backyard and took some pictures. And, and of course, he couldn't come because of his disability. So he was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] watching us go. [CRYING] My mother took us to the station. And we were at the station for quite a while. And all the kids were collected. And we were kind of separate, attached to a children's transport. And it became important because later on they held up the train because of our, because of us. And there was some question about whether we, they would allow us to continue or not. And finally, they, they let us continue. But I remember, remember them coming in and taking our passports and walking up--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some SS guys, they appeared and making a big issue of something. I don't know why. But apparently, you know, they're very meticulous with their papers and all that. But the people who were guiding the transport sort of convinced them that we are part of them. And they let us go, finally. So once we got into Holland, everybody gave a big cheer.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you knew where you were going.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your parents knew where you were going.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you scared?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I somehow looked upon it as sort of a big adventure. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't know what I really thought. I-- somehow, I felt that once I get to England, like, I'm going to do things. I'm going to be able to bring them over. [LAUGHS] I guess a kid's illusions are such that they feel they can do anything and everything. And I think I had that feeling, the realization-- I didn't have the realization of what really was going on and certainly had no inkling that this was going to be a final good-bye.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened when you arrived in England?

speaker: interviewee
text: We arrived at Liverpool Street Station. And this [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] business associate of my father's was there to pick us up. And they took us to the Griggs' home in northwest London.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was their name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Griggs. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] kind of introduced us and left us there. That, that was pretty much it. I think we saw them again on one, on one occasion.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when did you arrive?

speaker: interviewee
text: 22nd of June, 1939. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we were enrolled in school immediately. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you know, we couldn't speak any English. I didn't know a word of English. But somehow, sign language seemed to do. And I don't even know how it came. But the language came easily and quickly, especially when you have no, no alternative. But I think the age has a great deal to do with it. And I'd go to the school. And they'd have composition. And she would tell me to write something in German. And she would mark it A or-- [LAUGHS] she, she couldn't read it, either.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was, it was fine. I mean, I felt, I felt a little strange. I remember once looking out the window in the house. And we lived in a single-family home, one of those traditional old English homes. And down below, there was a group of Black Shirts. And they were singing the Nazi hymn. What are Black Shirts?

speaker: interviewee
text: Black Shirts was a Nazi party in England that was outlawed during the war. But at that time, there were-- the war wasn't on. And it was difficult, coming from Vienna, where everything is sort of black or white, seeing democracy in action, that here on one side, they could take us in as refugees and offer us a haven. Yet on the other hand, you had these guys demonstrating around and singing these songs. And it was hard thing for, for a kid to grasp that. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I, I remember that very vividly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about school in England.

speaker: interviewee
text: People were-- the teachers were all nice. The kids were nice. It wasn't long that after I came there that we had summer vacation. And then, all of a sudden-- I think that the winds of war were kind of really brewing up there-- that we were going to be evacuated by school. And so we were evacuated to the country, about 30 miles north of London. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My sister and I managed to stay together, even though we actually went to different schools. They arranged that, which I think was very nice. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The only thing that wasn't nice was the family that we went to. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I guess it wasn't easy for the families, either. It was a young couple. I think she was only in her late 20s, for a young married couple. And suddenly, they're-- say, they gave them a choice. You can either get a couple of evacuees or a family from London that's being evacuated or a couple of soldiers billeted because there was some station near there. And it was according to how many rooms you had in the house. And they, they had to go for one of those. So they chose the lesser of these three evils, which they thought they'll get a couple of kids. Well, when they saw who they got, they-- they didn't like Jews. The woman told me many times that she thought the best thing that ever happened was what Hitler was doing to the Jews, although at that time I don't think she really knew what was happening to the Jews. But she just knew what bad things are happening. And I had strict rules that I was only allowed inside the house for meals and to sleep. And I had to take the dog for a walk before going to school. So most of the time, I-- because English weather can be pretty miserable-- I used to hang around the doorways of shops, asking people the time, so I knew when I could, could go back into the house. And didn't have a proper bed to sleep in. I had just one of those army cots with just a sheet on it, nothing underneath it, and a blanket over the top of me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But the strange thing about it is there were so many nice people there who would have offered me a home and wanted me to stay there. What I didn't realize is that I had the power within me to complain about it. You know, all I had to do was say to somebody, you know, I don't like it there. I mean, there were kids who were switching around constantly. There were other kids-- the parents came down and poked around in the room and said, I'm not having my child stay in this place and take them out of there. Well, nobody came down to us. And I didn't think that I had the right or the power to say anything. I stayed there for three years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your sister with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was there for two years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how was she treated? Same?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was treated perhaps a little better but not a whole lot better. But, but that wasn't all bad. I'm just giving you the environment of the house, of this particular house. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The school was asked to help participate in potato picking and things like that in the farms. So I went there. And the farmer and the family welcomed me, and I continued going to the farm, working there part-time after, after school. And that became like a home away from home. I was welcomed there all the time, and they used to invite me to Christmas parties. And, and I had lots of friends amongst the kids. So it was a stern, kind of spartan living condition in the house. But outside of that, I had-- I think I had a nice time with kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: In England, did you celebrate any of the Jewish holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not during that period. During that period, I didn't even know when they were, any Jewish holidays. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I used to go out carol singing. And although I never thought-- nobody ever tried to convert me. And I never thought of myself as being Christian, but I enjoyed it. It was just a lot of fun. I was together with the kids. And we, we enjoyed it. I, I, I guess because of that, I still enjoy listening to the music this time of the year. I don't look upon it in threatening in any way. I have good memories from that period.

speaker: interviewer
text: That may make today even more special. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] OK. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] So now you're about 10 years old?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, 10 to, to 14, I was there all that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were in England, did you remember the things that you ate in particular?

speaker: interviewee
text: In England?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Again, I hate to chastise the whole British population based on the couple that I stayed with. This person didn't have a clue of cooking. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I would find things like a pig's ear with hair still on it on a plate in front of me and expected to eat that. And I used to spit things into my handkerchief and, and let it out on the street as I took the dog for a walk. She only knew one thing-- you boil everything. I think, I mean, England is not known for its kind of cooking anyway. But this was, this was rock bottom.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the whole family eat that food or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was only her husband and my sister and I. My sister somehow used to swallow things. And I'd see her face peering at me because she knew what I was doing. And-- but that's, that's the way I survived there. But I used to-- at the farm, I used to have milk. I used to have cheese there. And vegetables I could always manage anyway. But the food there was pretty bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were at school, did any of the children [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] make you feel different because you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Well, first of all, at this time, you know, it wasn't long-- I don't know how long it took for me to grasp the language and speak it with the same London accent as the others but really not long at all. I didn't practice any Jewish holidays while I was in that. Although they may have known I was Jewish, I, I never had the slightest problem. That was never an issue there, and I, I had lots of friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you in contact with your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was only, only had letters for about a, a year. And then that stopped. Then afterwards, we received via the Red Cross 25 words, like a, like a telegram message. And I think we received one of those. And that was the end of it. By 1940, '41, there was no more contact.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there ever any anti-Semitism at all when you were in England?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'd have to, I'd have to spring forward to the army for that.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Well, we'll get there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So put that off when you get there.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were in England staying with this family, were you ever scared?

speaker: interviewee
text: Physically? No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mentally?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, that-- I mean, she, she hit me once or twice. But I was never scared that anything drastic would happen to me or anything like that, no. It was just, you know, I, I feel worse about it now when I look back than I did at the time. Because when I look back now, I, I can see how easily those three years could have been so pleasant, as opposed to being so miserable and all because of not saying anything. And at the time, I just took things the way they came. It's later on, you look back, and you-- I kind of feel bad that you had those experiences what needn't have been there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in '43-- the end of '43 or so, went back to London [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and finished school there the following year. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go back by yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My sister had already gone back. The-- Mr. Griggs' wife died just after the war broke out. So I hardly remember her. And my sister finished up, actually, being a housekeeper at the age of 16 for Mr. Griggs and a lodger who lived in that house. So although, you know, this was not their grand design to bring kids over here in order that they could help them, it, in actual fact, turned out that way.
